career_angle: Learning tinygrad's codebase is genuinely useful for understanding ML compilers and GPU programming at a low level. Don't bet your career on the ecosystem though -- PyTorch and JAX still own hiring pipelines.

dimensions.name: Unknown
dimensions.rationale: Ben's Bites repackaging a company's own marketing page as a story. No independent analysis or verification.

dimensions.name: Unknown
dimensions.rationale: Hardware specs for shipping products are concrete. But 'fastest growing' is unsubstantiated, and exabox specs are projections for unreleased hardware.

dimensions.name: Unknown
dimensions.rationale: tinygrad is open source on GitHub, framework claims are verifiable by reading the code. tinybox red has shipped to real customers.

dimensions.name: Unknown
dimensions.rationale: This IS the company's own sales page. They're selling $15K+ hardware. Every claim serves their commercial interest.

dimensions.name: Unknown
dimensions.rationale: tinygrad has a real community but remains niche. No serious ML lab has adopted it over PyTorch/JAX. The '3 OpTypes' simplification is elegant but unproven at scale.

dimensions.name: Unknown
dimensions.rationale: Hardware specs are admirably specific. Software claims ('simple and powerful', 'fastest growing') are pure marketing fog with no metrics.

dimensions.name: Unknown
dimensions.rationale: George Hotz shipped comma.ai and tinybox red (with delays). But history of ambitious timelines that slip. Exabox is a massive leap from 4-GPU boxes.
